From Step 5, Manager(james) → LunchInCompany(james). So if James IS a manager, he DOES have lunch in the company. From Step 10, if James is NOT a manager, he does NOT have lunch in the company. Premise 7 does not force James to be a non-manager, so the conclusion depends on whether James is a manager.]
FOL: (Manager(james) → LunchInCompany(james)) ∧ (¬Manager(james) → ¬LunchInCompany(james))

STEP 12: [Since Premise 7 allows James to be either a manager (with AppearInCompany) or not a manager (without AppearInCompany), and we cannot determine which case holds, the truth of ¬LunchInCompany(james) is uncertain.]
FOL: ¬(Manager(james) ↔ ¬Manager(james))

ANSWER: Uncertain

STEP 9: [From Premise 2 contrapositive: if James is an employee and did not schedule a meeting, he did not have lunch in the company.]
FOL: (Employee(james) ∧ ¬ScheduleMeeting(james)) → ¬LunchInCompany(james)

STEP 10: [Chaining Steps 7, 8, 9 with employee condition: if James is an employee and not a manager, then he does not have lunch in the company.]
FOL: (Employee(james) ∧ ¬Manager(james)) → ¬LunchInCompany(james)

STEP 11: [From Steps 5 and 10: if James is an employee, then being a manager leads to lunch in company, and not being a manager leads to no lunch in company. Premise 7 allows either case, so the conclusion is uncertain.]
FOL: Employee(james) → (LunchInCompany(james) ↔ Manager(james))
